meta.title: API & Integration Testing
meta.description: API testing, contract testing, integration testing with Postman, supertest, REST Assured, and Pact

items.text: Are correct HTTP status codes tested for success and error cases?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test that endpoints return correct status codes: 200 for success, 201 for creation, 400 for bad input, 401 for unauthorized, 404 for not found, 422 for validation errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong status codes break API clients. A 200 with an error body means the client treats errors as success.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that tests assert on status codes, not just response body. Verify error cases return appropriate 4xx/5xx codes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test only checks the response body. Also assert expect(response.status).toBe(201) for the creation endpoint.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always assert status codes. 200 vs 201 vs 204 matters to API consumers.

items.text: Is the response schema validated (not just individual fields)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validate the entire response structure against a JSON schema. Check required fields, types, and nested objects.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Checking individual fields misses added or removed fields. Schema validation catches structural changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for JSON schema validation (ajv, joi, zod). Verify the schema matches the API documentation.
baseContent.exampleComment: This only checks response.body.name. If the API removes 'email' or adds unexpected fields, the test won't catch it.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate response schemas, not just individual fields. Catch structural changes automatically.

items.text: Are error responses tested for proper format and messages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test that error responses have consistent format: error code, message, field-level validation errors, request ID.
baseContent.whyItMatters: API clients depend on consistent error formats to show user-friendly messages. Inconsistent errors break client error handling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Send invalid requests and verify the error response format. Check for field-level errors, error codes, and helpful messages.
baseContent.exampleComment: Sending an invalid email returns a generic 400 'Bad Request'. Return { errors: [{ field: 'email', message: 'Invalid email format' }] }.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test error responses as thoroughly as success responses. Consistent error format is an API contract.

items.text: Are Content-Type headers tested for requests and responses?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Verify that the API sets correct Content-Type headers and rejects requests with wrong Content-Type.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing Content-Type headers cause parsing failures. Accepting any Content-Type can lead to security issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that responses include correct Content-Type. Test sending requests with wrong Content-Type (text/plain to a JSON endpoint).
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint accepts text/plain but expects JSON body. Add Content-Type validation and return 415 Unsupported Media Type.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test Content-Type headers. Reject requests with unexpected Content-Type.

items.text: Are pagination, sorting, and filtering tested?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test list endpoints with pagination params (page, limit, offset), sorting (sort=name:asc), and filtering (status=active).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Pagination bugs are common: off-by-one errors, missing last page, wrong total count. These affect UI rendering.
baseContent.howToVerify: Test first page, last page, empty page, page beyond data. Verify total count, page metadata, and sorting order.
baseContent.exampleComment: This tests page 1 only. Add tests for: last page, empty results, page 0, page beyond max, different sort orders.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test pagination boundaries: first, last, empty, beyond-max. Verify total counts and sorting.

items.text: Are endpoints tested for proper authentication enforcement?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test that protected endpoints reject unauthenticated requests with 401. Verify expired tokens are rejected.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing authentication checks expose data to anyone. This is OWASP #1: Broken Access Control.
baseContent.howToVerify: Call every protected endpoint without a token. Verify 401 response. Test with expired and invalid tokens.
baseContent.exampleComment: GET /api/users returns 200 without any authentication token. This endpoint must require a valid auth token.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test that every protected endpoint returns 401 without authentication.

items.text: Are authorization rules tested (role-based, resource-based)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test that users can only access their own data and perform allowed operations. Verify role restrictions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) lets users access others' data by changing IDs. Authorization tests catch this.
baseContent.howToVerify: Authenticate as user A and try to access user B's resources. Verify 403 or 404 response.
baseContent.exampleComment: Authenticated as user A, GET /api/users/B/orders returns user B's orders. Must return 403 Forbidden.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test horizontal (user-to-user) and vertical (role-based) authorization for every endpoint.

items.text: Is rate limiting tested?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test that rate-limited endpoints return 429 Too Many Requests after exceeding limits. Verify retry-after headers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting, APIs are vulnerable to brute force attacks and abuse. Tests verify limits are enforced.
baseContent.howToVerify: Send requests exceeding the rate limit. Verify 429 response with appropriate retry-after header.
baseContent.exampleComment: The login endpoint has no rate limiting test. Send 100 requests in 10 seconds and verify rate limiting kicks in.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test rate limiting on sensitive endpoints (login, registration, password reset).

items.text: Are consumer-driven contract tests in place for service-to-service APIs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Pact, Spring Cloud Contract, or similar to verify API contracts between services. Consumer defines expectations, provider verifies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without contract tests, provider changes break consumers silently. Contract tests catch breaking changes before deployment.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for contract test files (Pact, OpenAPI). Verify both consumer and provider sides are tested.
baseContent.exampleComment: Service A depends on Service B's /users endpoint but there's no contract test. Add a Pact contract for this dependency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use contract tests between services. Consumer defines, provider verifies.

items.text: Are API changes tested for backward compatibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New API versions must not break existing clients. Adding fields is safe. Removing or renaming fields is breaking.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking API changes cause production outages for all consumers. Even internal APIs have multiple consumers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for removed fields, renamed fields, changed types, or removed endpoints. These are breaking changes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This renames 'user_name' to 'username' in the response. This breaks all existing clients. Add 'username' and keep 'user_name'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never remove or rename API fields. Add new fields alongside old ones. Deprecate gradually.

items.text: Does the API implementation match the OpenAPI/Swagger spec?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validate that actual API responses match the documented OpenAPI specification. Use automated spec testing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Documentation drift makes API specs useless. Consumers build against the spec, but the implementation differs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Use tools like Dredd, Schemathesis, or openapi-backend to validate responses against the spec automatically.
baseContent.exampleComment: The OpenAPI spec says 'email' is required, but the API returns responses without it. Fix the API or update the spec.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate API responses against OpenAPI specs automatically. Catch documentation drift.

items.text: Is API versioning strategy tested?

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the API is versioned (v1, v2), test that old versions still work correctly and new versions have expected changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking old API versions without notice strands clients on unsupported versions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run tests against all supported API versions. Verify deprecated endpoints return proper warnings.
baseContent.exampleComment: API v1 is still listed as supported but has no tests. Add tests to verify v1 continues to work with the latest code.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test all supported API versions. Verify deprecated versions return sunset headers.

items.text: Do integration tests verify database state changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: After API calls that modify data, verify the database state matches expectations. Don't just check the API response.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An API can return 200 but fail to persist data. Integration tests should verify the full round-trip.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that POST/PUT/DELETE tests query the database afterward to verify changes were persisted correctly.
baseContent.exampleComment: The create user test checks the API response but doesn't verify the user exists in the database. Add a database assertion.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Verify database state after write operations. API response alone isn't proof of persistence.

items.text: Are integration tests isolated with transaction rollback?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Wrap each integration test in a database transaction and roll back afterward. This keeps tests isolated and fast.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rollback, tests accumulate data and interfere with each other. Cleanup scripts are fragile and slow.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for transaction management in test setup/teardown. Verify the database is clean between tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: Tests insert data without rollback. After 50 runs, there are 5000 test records. Use transactions with rollback.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use transaction rollback for test isolation. Faster and more reliable than manual cleanup.

items.text: Are third-party service calls mocked (not calling real external APIs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mock external service calls (payment, email, SMS) in integration tests. Use WireMock, MSW, or nock for HTTP mocking.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Real third-party calls are slow, costly, and flaky. A test that sends real emails or charges real credit cards is dangerous.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for real HTTP calls to external services. Verify payment, email, and notification services are mocked.
baseContent.exampleComment: This integration test calls the real Stripe API. Mock it with nock/WireMock to avoid charges and flakiness.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mock external services. Test your integration with them using contract tests separately.

items.text: Are real databases used via test containers (not in-memory substitutes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use Testcontainers to run real database instances (PostgreSQL, Redis, MongoDB) in Docker for integration tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In-memory databases (H2, SQLite) behave differently from production databases. SQL syntax, constraints, and behavior differ.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests use in-memory database substitutes. Verify integration tests use the same database engine as production.
baseContent.exampleComment: Tests use H2 instead of PostgreSQL. H2 doesn't support PostgreSQL-specific features. Use Testcontainers for a real PostgreSQL.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Testcontainers for real database testing. In-memory substitutes miss database-specific bugs.

items.text: Is test data managed with fixtures or factories (not hardcoded)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use fixture files or factory functions to create test data. Don't hardcode JSON payloads throughout test files.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded test data is duplicated and hard to update. Factories provide defaults with easy overrides.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for large inline JSON objects in tests. These should use shared factories or fixtures.
baseContent.exampleComment: This 30-line JSON payload is copy-pasted in 10 tests. Create a userFactory() that returns defaults with overrides.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use factories for test data. Avoid copy-pasting JSON payloads across tests.

items.text: Are tests idempotent (safe to run multiple times)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Running tests twice should produce the same results. Tests shouldn't fail on the second run due to leftover data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-idempotent tests fail on re-run: unique constraint violations, already-exists errors, stale data from previous run.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run the test suite twice without resetting the database. If the second run fails, tests aren't idempotent.
baseContent.exampleComment: Second run fails with 'email already exists'. Use unique test data per run or clean up in beforeEach.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tests must be idempotent. Use unique data or clean up before/after each test.

items.text: Are response time assertions included for critical endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Assert that critical endpoints respond within acceptable time limits. Catch performance regressions early.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A query that goes from 50ms to 5s won't be caught by functional tests. Response time assertions catch regressions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for response time assertions on critical endpoints. Verify thresholds are reasonable for the test environment.
baseContent.exampleComment: The user search endpoint should respond within 200ms. Add expect(response.time).toBeLessThan(200) to catch regressions.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add response time assertions for critical endpoints. Catch performance regressions in tests.

items.text: Are edge cases tested (large payloads, special characters, unicode)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test with large request bodies, special characters, unicode, empty strings, and very long strings to catch edge cases.
baseContent.whyItMatters: APIs often break with unexpected input: emoji in names, 10MB payloads, null bytes, or zero-width characters.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tests with unicode input, very long strings, empty arrays, and large payloads.
baseContent.exampleComment: No tests with unicode names. Add a test with name: '🧑‍💻 Developer éàü' to verify unicode handling.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test edge cases: unicode, empty input, large payloads, special characters.
